Just now

--

Your coding agent reached into the browser’s saved passwords and enumerated the Windows credential store. To the endpoint agent watching that machine, that is textbook credential theft, and it did exactly what it was built to do: it blocked it.
Click here to read this article for free
The tool you trust is tripping the alarm built for attackers
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sophos pulled seven days of behavioral telemetry off Windows machines in June 2026 and counted, by unique machine, what its endpoint engine was blocking. A lot of it was not malware. It was Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenAI Codex doing their jobs.
The split is stark. Credential access accounted for 56.2% of the blocked activity, and execution another 28.8%. These are not fringe rules. They are the core of what an EDR exists to catch.
The single biggest credential-access rule, 42.6% of that category, fires when a process uses the Windows Data Protection API to decrypt the browser’s stored credential data. Claude Code hit it by running PowerShell that called DPAPI to unlock saved browser data. Contextually it was browser automation. Structurally it was indistinguishable from an infostealer.
